segments.s222-g137: بمردی is an adverbial/qualifying phrase ('in manliness' / 'as a man') placed before the predicate; نباید شد is an impersonal modal construction where نباید expresses prohibition/obligation ('one should not') and شد here functions as the verbal/copular element ('be / become') — together meaning 'one must not be' or 'one should not become'. اندر گمان is a prepositional phrase ('in the thought/belief') that completes the clause: literally 'one should not (be) in the belief.' The word order is slightly poetic (qualifier + modal + copula + prepositional phrase).
segments.s222-g138: که introduces a subordinate nominal clause ('that ...'). بر تو is the prepositional object ('upon/against you'); درازست is a predicative adjective meaning 'is long/extended' and appears before its subject because of poetic inversion; دست زمان ('the hand of Time') is the subject (دست = 'hand' or figuratively 'power/agency', زمان = 'Time'), so the literal order reads 'upon you long is the hand of Time.' The whole clause says 'that Time's hand reaches far upon you.'
segments.s222-g139: بپایان شد is a fixed/poetic verbal phrase meaning 'came to an end' (بپایان here marking the ending + شد 'became/happened'); the verb phrase precedes the noun for poetic inversion. این رزم is the noun phrase 'this battle' and functions as the subject/undergoer of 'came to an end'. کاموس گرد appears as a proper name (a compound: 'Kāmos-gird' or similar) placed immediately after the clause; here کاموس and گرد should be read together as a name rather than as the dictionary/ocean or 'around' senses listed, hence their senses are unspecified (-1).
segments.s222-g140: همی شد که is a conventional narrative formula: همی is an archaic/poetic particle that helps narrate or emphasize and شد here means 'it happened' (so 'it came to pass that ...'). The following verbless repetition/parallelism 'جان آورد جان ببرد' strings two short clauses together: جان آورد uses آورد (past) with جان 'life' meaning 'life was brought/given' (a polyvalent idiom), and جان ببرد uses the present/subjunctive form ببرد of برد meaning 'takes/take away life.' Grammatically this is a compact, poetic coordination where the object 'جان' is repeated and two verbs present contrasting or successive aspects (one bringing life, one taking life), i.e. 'life was given and life was taken.'
segments.s222-g65: بزد (past) + نیزه is a simple verb + object: 'he struck (with) the spear.' The following و links a second verb phrase; برگرفتش is برگرفت + enclitic -ش (third-person object) meaning 'he took/ seized him.' ز زین is a short prepositional phrase 'from the saddle' (ز = poetic 'from', placed before زین).
segments.s222-g66: بینداخت is simple past 'he threw/cast (down)'; آسان here functions adverbially 'easily' or 'lightly' modifying that verb. بروی زمین expresses direction 'onto the ground' (بروی = بر + روی/onto, زمین = ground). The whole clause says he cast him easily onto the earth.
segments.s222-g67: عنان را گران کرد is a causative/perfective phrase: object عنان + را, then گران کرد 'made (it) heavy' = 'pulled the reins tight' (کرد here = caused/made). The conjunction و links the next clause; او را بنعل places the object (او را) before the instrumental noun بنعل ('horseshoe') in preparation for the verb that follows.
segments.s222-g68: همی کوفت is imperfective-past with همی showing continuous or repeated action: 'he kept striking' (کوفت = struck). تا introduces a result clause 'until'; خاک او کرد لعل is a causative turn of phrase: خاک او 'his dust' + کرد (made) + لعل (ruby) → 'he struck him until his dust was turned to ruby' (i.e., blood-red).
segments.s222-g69: تهمتن is the hero's name/epithet. ز here means 'from' or 'because of' and الوای (a shout/cry/interjection) is the cause: 'because of the shout.' شد دردمند is 'became distressed/in pain' (شد = became, دردمند = pained).
segments.s222-g70: ز فتراک gives the source/location 'from the saddle-cloth' (ز = from, فتراک = saddle-cloth). بگشاد is past 'he opened/unfastened' (ب- prefix + گشاد). پیچان modifies کمند: 'پیچان کمند' = 'the twisting/coiled lasso.' Together: 'he loosened/unfastened the coiled lasso from the saddle-cloth.'
segments.s222-g71: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' or 'as.' آهنگ here = 'intention/attack/aim' and جنگ سران = 'battle against the chiefs' (جنگ = battle, سران = leaders/chiefs). داشتی is second-person past 'you had/you held' — so the clause means 'when you intended battle against the chiefs.'
segments.s222-g72: This is a simple nominal list with a repeated verb: کمندی و گرزی گران داشتی = 'you had a lasso and a heavy mace.' کمندی is the lasso (object), و links, گرزی = mace/club modified by postpositive adjective گران 'heavy', and داشتی repeats the past-tense 'you had.'
segments.s222-g25: بقلب اندرون طوس نوذر بپای – The phrase orders location before the person and verb in a poetic way: 'بقلب اندرون طوس' = 'in the inner heart/inside of Tūs' (بقلب + اندرون giving a reinforced locative), then the name 'نوذر' and the predicate 'بپای' (a classical/poetic verb meaning to stand/lie in wait or be stationed). 'بپای' here is a past/steady-state narrative sense ('was positioned/stood ready') and links as the main verbal predicate for 'نوذر'.
segments.s222-g26: به پیش سپه کوس با کرنای – 'به پیش' functions as a locative/adverbial 'in front of' or 'at the head of'; 'سپه' is the head noun 'army'; 'کوس با کرنای' means 'with drum and horn/trumpet'. Word order places the location/role ('به پیش سپه') before the instruments ('کوس با کرنای'), and the preposition 'با' marks accompaniment ('with').
segments.s222-g27: همی دود آتش برآمد ز آب – 'همی' is a narrative/emphatic particle (here 'thereupon/indeed/just'), 'دود آتش' is the noun phrase 'smoke of fire' (genitive/attributive order), and 'برآمد' is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'; 'ز آب' ('from the water') is the source. The clause is a straightforward verbal sentence but with poetic inversion (noun before verb).
segments.s222-g28: نبیند چنین رزم جنگی بخواب – 'نبیند' is a negative present/general statement ('does not see' / 'one does not see'); 'چنین' = 'such'; 'رزم جنگی' = 'a battle/war (warlike combat)' with 'جنگی' adjectival; 'بخواب' is the adverbial expression 'in sleep' (ب‑ prefixed to خواب), so the clause means 'one does not see such a battle while asleep.' The verb is generic/present, giving a proverbial or eyewitness tone.
segments.s222-g29: برآمد ز هر سوی لشکر خروش – 'برآمد' (arose/issued forth) is the verb placed first for emphasis; 'ز هر سوی' = 'from every direction/side'; 'لشکر خروش' is a genitive phrase 'the army's roar' (army + roar). The clause describes the source and scope of the sound with poetic fronting of the verb.
segments.s222-g30: همی پیل را زان بدرید گوش – 'همی' again provides narrative emphasis; 'پیل را' (elephant + definite object marker را) marks the elephant as the affected object; 'زان' ('from that' / 'therefore') refers back to the preceding roar; 'بدرید' is the past verb 'tore' (3rd-person past); 'گوش' (ear) is the noun receiving the action. Word order places the object before the verb, as is common in Persian with را.
segments.s222-g31: نخستین که آمد میان دو صف – 'نخستین' = 'the first'; 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; 'آمد' is the past 'came'; 'میان دو صف' is the locative 'between two ranks/lines'. The head noun (نخستین) is followed by its relative clause, a standard classical Persian pattern.
segments.s222-g32: ز خون جگر بر لب آورده کف – 'ز' = 'from'; 'خون جگر' literally 'blood of the liver' (an idiomatic phrase for blood from the vital part); 'بر لب' = 'upon the lip'; 'آورده' is the perfect/past participle 'brought/has brought', used here predicatively ('he had brought/was bearing'); 'کف' = 'foam' (i.e., foam on the lip). The clause is a compact past-participial description of the warrior's appearance.
segments.s222-g33: This is a simple nominal clause with a past copula: 'سپهبد سرافراز کاموس بود' = 'the triumphant spahbed was Kamus.' 'سپهبد' (spahbed) is the head noun, 'سرافراز' is an attributive adjective placed after the noun (common in Persian poetry), 'کاموس' is the proper name following the noun phrase, and 'بود' is the past copula agreeing with that NP.
segments.s222-g34: 'که' introduces a relative clause describing Kamus. The clause lists his retinue: 'با لشکر و پیل و با کوس بود' literally 'was with an army and elephants and with war‑drums.' Each 'با' marks accompaniment/possession and the final 'بود' is the copula in the relative clause; the coordinating 'و' simply links the items.
segments.s222-g35: 'همی برخروشید' is a classical/perhaps iterative past: 'he roared/thundered forth' (همی adds an old poetic progressive/emphatic flavor). 'چون' introduces a simile 'like', followed by 'پیل' (elephant) and the adjective 'مست' (figuratively 'intoxicated' or 'madly violent') describing the manner of the roaring.
segments.s222-g36: Elliptical descriptive phrase: 'یکی' ('one' / 'a certain one') introduces an individual; 'گرزه' (mace) is the instrument, 'گاو پیکر' is a compound adjective 'cow/bull‑bodied' (i.e. of bull‑like build) modifying that person, and 'بدست' ('in hand') indicates that the mace was held. The verb 'held' is omitted but understood from context (a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s222-g37: This is the question 'کجاست' (= 'where is') formed here by the demonstrative NP followed by the copula: 'آن جنگجوی پیاده کجاست' = 'Where is that foot‑warrior?' 'آن' (that) + 'جنگجوی' (warrior) + post‑nominal adjective 'پیاده' (on foot/infantry), then the enclitic copula 'ست' attached to 'کجا' gives 'is where.'
segments.s222-g38: A relative/explanatory clause introduced by 'که': 'که از نامداران چنین رزم خواست' = 'who, of the renowned, demanded/asked for such a battle.' 'از نامداران' means 'from among the famous' (i.e. one of the renowned), 'چنین' = 'such', 'رزم' is the object 'fight/battle', and 'خواست' is past tense of 'خواستن' used here in the sense 'asked/demanded' (requested the duel).
segments.s222-g39: 'کنون‌گر' is an archaic conditional particle meaning roughly 'if now' (classical poetic usage). 'بیاید' is the present/subjunctive form 'he should come' or 'he comes' used in the protasis of the conditional, and 'به آوردگاه' is the prepositional phrase 'to the battlefield.'
segments.s222-g40: The clause 'تهی ماند از تیر او جایگاه' has an inverted, poetic word order: 'تهی ماند' = 'remained empty' (past), 'از تیر او' = 'of/by his arrow' (here 'از ... تهی ماند' = 'became devoid of ...'), and 'جایگاه' is the subject 'the place' placed at the end for emphasis. So literally 'the place remained empty of his arrow.'
segments.s222-g57: مشو is a prohibitive (negative imperative) meaning “do not become / do not be.” غرق is the predicate participle “drowned / overwhelmed.” ز + noun gives the cause/source (“from / by / because of”), so ز آب هنرهای خویش literally means “by the water of your arts” — i.e. being overwhelmed by one’s own skills. The possessive is split: خوی (root) plus enclitic ش marking “his/her/one’s.”
segments.s222-g58: نگه‌دار here functions as an imperative/command “keep / hold / preserve” (classical form often written as the compound نگه دار). بر is the preposition “on/upon.” جایگه (literally “place/position”) is the noun modified by the following adjective (or simply the object), and پای خویش = “your foot/footing,” with خویش expressed by خوی + enclitic ش. The clause therefore means “keep your foot upon your place” = maintain your position.
segments.s222-g59: چو is the comparative/simile particle “like / as / when.” قطره is the simile’s term “a drop.” بر is the preposition “upon/into.” ژرف (adjective) precedes دریا to give “the deep sea.” بری here is an attribute of the sea (poetic/archaic), translated as “wild/untamed” or “vast,” so the whole phrase reads “like a drop upon the deep (wild) sea.”
segments.s222-g60: This clause has inverted word order for emphasis: ماند (past tense “remained / proved”) comes before the subject این داوری (“this judgment”). بدیوانگی is a predicate noun “madness / insanity,” so ماند بدیوانگی = “remained/turned into madness.” The demonstrative این points back to the judgment just mentioned.
segments.s222-g61: شد is the past auxiliary/verb “became / happened / arose.” الوای functions as an exclamatory/nominal element here — “a cry / proclamation” (poetic). آهنگ means “intention / aim,” so الوای آهنگ = “a cry/proclamation of intent.” کاموس is a proper name (Kāmus); کرد is the verb “made / did,” here taking الوای آهنگ as its object: “(a cry) was made of Kamus’s intent.”
segments.s222-g62: که introduces the subordinate clause giving the content/reason (“for / that / who”). جوید is a present-form verb meaning “seeks / desires / strives.” بآورد (آورد with the prefix بـ) is an infinitival/present sense “to bring / to cause,” taking با او نبرد (“battle with him”) as its object: he seeks to bring about a fight with him. Word order places the verb forms before the object for emphasis.
segments.s222-g63: نهادند is a past-tense 3rd‑person plural verb “they placed / they set up.” آوردگاهی is the direct object meaning “a place of bringing,” i.e. poetically a battlefield or arena; بزرگ is the adjective “great / large” modifying آوردگاهی. The structure is straightforward subject‑verb‑object with adjective following the noun it modifies.
segments.s222-g64: کشانی (from کشاند) here functions as a modifier/agentive idea meaning a bringing/force that draws in — used with بیامد (“came”) to mean “a (driving) host/force came.” بیامد is simple past “came.” بکردار literally means “with/in (a) manner/behavior,” and گرگ (“wolf”) follows as the model of that behavior: “came with wolf-like conduct,” i.e. behaving like wolves.
segments.s222-g113: مبادا که is a fixed negative optative: 'may it not be that / God forbid that'. کین is the subject of آورد ('vengeance'); آورد here is a past/present-tense narrative verb meaning 'bring/cause'. سرفراز is a predicative adjective 'victorious / proud' (object implied: 'him'), so the clause means 'may it not be that vengeance makes him proud.' Word order: neg.optative + subordinate particle که + subject + verb + predicate.
segments.s222-g114: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). بس زود is an adverbial intensifier phrase meaning 'very soon' (بس intensifies زود). بیند is subjunctive/present form 'he will see' (here narrative future sense). نشیب و فراز is a paired nominal phrase 'descent and ascent' = 'the ups and downs'; the direct object of بیند.
segments.s222-g115: دو دست is the direct object ('two hands'). از پس پشت is a prepositional expression 'from/behind the back' (پس here 'behind/after' modifying پشت). بستش is the verb 'he bound/fastened it/him' with the enclitic -ش marking the object. چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone' describing how the binding was done. Word order: object + locative phrase + verb(+clitic) + simile.
segments.s222-g116: آورد is the past/ narrative 'he brought/put'. اندر is the preposition 'into/within' and takes کمند ('snare/loop') as its object; چنگ ('claw') is the thing brought into the snare. بخم is an archaic/obscure modifier here (listed as archaic) that likely colours the action; grammatically it precedes the phrase. Overall sequence: [archaic modifier] + object (کمند) + locative (اندر) + verb (آورد) + direct object (چنگ).
segments.s222-g117: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. خرامان is an adverb/adjunct 'with a proud/stately gait' modifying بیامد. بایران here functions as a modifier or name attached to سپاه (either 'of Bayrān' or a descriptive epithet); سپاه is the noun 'army/host'. Word order: verb + manner adjunct + modifier + head noun.
segments.s222-g118: بزیر کش is a compound verbal idea meaning 'drag/pull down' (بزیر 'downwards' + کش 'pull/drag'). اندر تن is a locative phrase 'into/onto the body' or 'to the ground on his person' specifying where he was dragged. کینه‌خواه is the object 'the avenger / the vengeful one'. Order: direction+verb, locative phrase, direct object.
segments.s222-g119: بگردان likely functions here as a verb meaning 'turning' (a verbal preface) or an imperative-turned-narrative 'he turned'; چنین گفت is a common sequence 'thus said' meaning 'he said these words'. کین رزمجوی is an appositive/genitive phrase 'the vengeance-seeking warrior' (کین modifying رزمجوی). Word order: verb (turn) + manner adverb (thus) + گفت 'said' + appositive subject.
segments.s222-g120: ز بس is a causal/intensifying prepositional phrase 'because of so much / from so great'. زور و کین is a coordinated noun phrase 'strength and vengeance/hatred'. اندر آمد is the verbal phrase 'came in / came upon'; بروی here functions as a directional/targetive element ('upon you' / 'on your person')—so the clause means 'because of so much strength and hatred it came upon you.' Word order: causal marker + intensifier + coordinated nouns + locative verb phrase + target.
segments.s222-g97: سوار (the rider) is the subject placed at the start for emphasis; از دلیری is an ablative phrase 'from/out of bravery' giving the cause, with از used in the causal sense; بیفشارد is a past tense/poetic-form verb ‘pressed’ (ب + فشرد) taking ران (hindquarter/flank) as its object in direct order.
segments.s222-g98: This clause is two short verbless/state phrases linked by و. گران شد is a predicative phrase 'became heavy' (شد = became), with رکیب as its subject (here 'the rider/companion'); و introduces the second result: سبک شد عنان is 'the reins became light' (سبک شد = 'became light', عنان = 'reins'), so the sequence gives two resulting states.
segments.s222-g99: همی خواست is a habitual/past continuous idea ('he was repeatedly trying' — همی marks ongoing action, خواست is past 'wished/tried'); کان here = 'that' (archaic/demonstrative) introduces the object phrase خم خام کمند, a noun phrase 'the curve/bend of the raw noose' (خم = bend/curve, خام = raw/unripe, کمند = lasso/snare).
segments.s222-g100: بنیرو is an adverbial phrase 'with force/strength' (bi-niru), ز هم here functions as 'from/together' (از هم/ز هم) indicating separation, and بگسلاند is a past/transitive verb 'to loosen/tear apart' with ز بند ('from the bond/fetter') the phrase indicating what it is being torn from; the line describes an attempt to force the snare/bond apart.
segments.s222-g101: شد از هوش is an idiomatic sequence 'became out of awareness' = 'lost consciousness' (شد = became); کاموس in this position is unusual — it looks like a proper name or a poetic word and does not fit the supplied nominal senses, and نگسست خام follows as a second clause 'the raw one/part tore' (نگسست = broke/ruptured, خام = raw/unfinished), describing something tearing as he lost awareness.
segments.s222-g102: گو here functions as a naming/introducing element (literally 'called/so‑named' in poetry) followed by پیلتن (a place-name or personal name), then the object-marked phrase رخش را ('Rakhsh' + را marks it as definite object), and کرد رام is a causative/result phrase 'made (کرد) [it] tame (رام)'; overall: 'so‑called Piltan made Rakhsh tame.'
segments.s222-g103: عنان را uses را to mark 'the reins' as definite object, and بیچید is a past verb 'plucked/snapped/snatch‑pulled' (here 'he seized/snapped the reins'); و connects to the next clause, او را ز زین = 'him (object) from the saddle' (ز = from, زین = saddle), showing the direct object who is then removed from the saddle.
segments.s222-g104: نگون اندر آورد is a compound action phrase: نگون ('overthrown/downfallen') with اندر آورد ('brought in/caused into [that state]') meaning 'brought him down/overthrew him'; و زد بر زمین continues with زد 'struck' and بر زمین 'upon the ground', completing the sequence that he hurled and struck the man to the ground.
segments.s222-g73: بیامد (past tense) is a third-person past 'he came'. بغرید is a second past-form verb here giving a successive action 'he roared' (classical narrative often strings past verbs). چون پیل مست is a simile 'like an elephant drunk/intoxicated' with چون introducing the comparison and مست qualifying پیل.
segments.s222-g74: This is an instrumental/locative nominal phrase describing equipment: 'a lasso on (his) arm and a mace in (his) hand.' کمندی is the noun 'a lasso' (the -ی here forms the noun), به marks the relation/placement, بازو and دست are locations, and و coordinates the two items.
segments.s222-g75: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (direction/indirect object). گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as past narrative), and کاموس is the speaker's name; چندین مدم is an adverbial phrase 'for several moments/for a time.' The word order (بدو گفت کاموس) is inverted poetically — 'Kāmos said to him.'
segments.s222-g76: بنیروی is the prepositional phrase 'by the power/strength of' (بـ + نیروی). این رشته is the genitive phrase 'this rope/string' (رشته = rope/string), and شصت خم is a numeral phrase 'sixty bends/coils' with خم meaning 'bend/coil'. The whole phrase reports what Kamus asserts about the rope's turns.
segments.s222-g77: چنین پاسخ آورد is a fixed narrative formula 'gave such an answer' (چنین modifying پاسخ, آورد = brought/gave). رستم is the agent (Rostam). که introduces the following subordinate/thesis clause ('that / namely'). شیر begins that subordinate clause as its subject 'the lion.'
segments.s222-g78: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when' (چو = when/as). نخچیر is the object 'prey/game', بیند is a present/habitual 3rd-person form 'sees' (classical narrative often uses present forms for general truths), بغرد is the action of vocalizing (here 'bellows/roars') that follows, and دلیر is an adjective 'brave' describing how he bellows.
segments.s222-g79: نخستین 'first' is an adverb/chronological marker. بر + ین together function as 'برین' = 'this upon' (poetic/older orthography), so برین کینه = 'this grudge upon (you)'. بستی is 2nd-person past 'you bound/tied', and کمر indicates the place/girding 'around the waist' (i.e., you girded it). The clause is a past narrative accusing the addressee of first girding the grudge.
segments.s222-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking origin, ایران is the proper noun 'Iran', بکشتی here is 2nd-person past 'you killed', and یکی نامور is the object 'one famed (man)' with یکی indefinite and نامور 'renowned'. The clause states the consequential deed: you killed a famed man of Iran.
segments.s222-g49: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where'. نیزه is the noun 'spear' and is the thing being held. رستم او is a possessive sequence: the name رستم followed by the pronoun او functioning like a genitive marker ('his, of Rostam') — classical/poetic order where the possessor precedes the pronominal possessive. داشتی is the 2nd-person singular past form of داشتن ('you had/you were holding').
segments.s222-g50: پس is a connective 'then/so'. پشت او = 'his back / his rear' (پشت + genitive pronoun او). هیچ before a verb means 'nothing / not at all', and نگذاشتی is 2nd-singular past of گذاشتن (here 'to leave/allow'); with هیچ it yields 'you left nothing' (i.e. you left him no rear/support).
segments.s222-g51: بسی is an intensifier 'much/very'. رنج برده is a compound perfect/participial expression: رنج (suffering) + برده (past participle of بردن) = 'has suffered/has borne hardship'. بکار عنان is the prepositional/infinitival phrase 'in the use of the reins' (ب + کار = 'in/for the use', عنان = 'reins'), giving the domain in which he suffered or trained.
segments.s222-g52: بیاموخته here functions as a perfect participle 'has learned / has become skilled' (classical form of آموختن). It takes the following coordinated nouns گرز و تیر و سنان as its objects/fields of skill (mace, arrow, and spear). The verb comes at the end, a common classical word order.
segments.s222-g53: برنج here is problematic in the provided sense list (literal 'rice') but in context is poetic and functions as a noun indicating grief/distress; بسختی is an adverb 'with difficulty / severely'. جگر سوخته is an adjectival phrase 'liver burned' — an idiom meaning 'heart/scarcely enduring sorrow' or 'heart burned' (deep anguish). The three parts together describe emotional suffering.
segments.s222-g54: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. رستم is the source/agent. هنرها is the plural object 'arts/skills'. بیاموخته is the perfect participle 'has learned' (same form as in the previous line) concluding the clause: '(he) has learned the arts from Rostam.'
segments.s222-g55: بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him' (literally 'to him' = ب + او) marking the indirect object/recipient of speech. گفت is the past 'said' (reported-speech verb). که introduces the reported content. بیدار باش is an imperative 'be awake/alert' (بیدار + باش imperative).
segments.s222-g56: بآورد is from آوردن with the prefix ب- (here functioning as a jussive/imperative-like command) meaning 'bring / cause to bring'. این ترک is the direct object 'this Turk'. هشیار باش is another imperative 'be alert / be watchful'. The sequence reads as two commands: 'bring this Turk' and 'be on your guard.'
segments.s222-g9: ز بالای او is a causal/prepositional phrase placed before the verb: ز = “because of” (poetic). بالای او = “his height/loftiness” with the possessed noun (بالا -> بالای) followed by the pronoun او (possessor). آسمان is the subject and خیره گشت is a two-word predicate meaning “became transfixed/astonished,” with گشت (past of گشتن) functioning as the copular/auxiliary ‘became’.
segments.s222-g10: زمین is the subject. از پی is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in the wake of/after” or “because of the track of”; رخش او is a genitive construction where رخش = “his steed/warhorse” and او is the possessor. تیره گشت is the predicate “became dark,” again using گشت as the past/coprial verb. Word order is typical poetic inversion: subject – adverbial/prep phrase – predicate.
segments.s222-g11: برآمد is an intransitive verb “arose/issued forth.” ز هر دو سپه is the source phrase “from both hosts/armies” (ز = from; هر دو = the two/both). بوق و کوس are the instruments that ‘arose’ (trumpet and war-drum). This is a compact poetic order: verb – source – nominatives.
segments.s222-g12: Two short nominal clauses linked by asyndeton: زمین آهنین شد = “the earth became iron” (زمین subject, آهنین predicate adjective, شد = became). سپهر آبنوس follows as a parallel noun + adjective (“the heavens [became] ebony”), with the verb شد understood to govern both clauses by parallelism (a common poetic economy). آبنوس is used adjectivally here (= very dark/black).
segments.s222-g13: جهان is the subject and لرز لرزان شد is a reduplicative, intensive predicate: لرز (tremor) + لرزان (shaking) + شد (became) = “shook/trembled violently.” The following و دشت و کوه lists other affected locales (plain and mountain); the verb is omitted there by ellipsis, a frequent poetic device.
segments.s222-g14: زمین شد = “the earth became,” followed by a causal phrase ز نعل ستوران = “from/because of the hoofs of the steeds” (ز = from/because of; نعل = horseshoe/hoof; ستوران = herds/steeds). ستوه is a predicative adjective meaning “weary/tired,” so the whole means the earth was made weary by the pounding hoofs.
segments.s222-g15: This short clause uses an epithet + noun + locative: وزین is an epithet “dignified/weighty,” روی is “face” (here part of the epithet: ‘dignified-faced’), and کاموس is the noun being described. بر میمنه is the locative “on the right.” The verb is omitted (elliptical), typical of epic diction — something like “stood/was” is implied. Note: کاموس is an archaic/poetic word here; it may be a proper name or a metaphorical noun (the text uses it poetically), so the exact referent is context-dependent.
segments.s222-g16: پس پشت او = “behind him” (پس = after/behind; پشت = back/behind; او = his). ژنده پیل و بنه lists what is behind him: ژنده پیل = “a ragged/old elephant” (ژنده as adjective + پیل elephant) and بنه = “a small bundle/heap” (likely baggage or a piled-up load). The conjunction و links the two items; again, no copular verb is expressed and is understood.
segments.s222-g1: بپوشید is the classical epic form with the prefixed b- plus the past stem پوشید, functioning as a simple past third-person (“he put on”); the verb comes before the proper name (typical of Ferdowsi’s word order). رستم is the subject (proper name). سلیح نبرد is the object phrase ‘war-armament / battle-gear’ placed after the verb; سلیح here denotes armament and نبرد specifies its martial use.
segments.s222-g2: به آوردگه رفت is a short clause: به + آوردگه gives the direction/place (‘to the venue/place of arrival’), and رفت is the past 3rd-person of رفتن (‘he went’). با introduces an accompanying instrument/companion (‘with’). داروبرد here is a noun following با describing what he carried/was accompanied by; its precise lexical sense is unclear in the supplied senses, so it is left unspecified.
segments.s222-g3: This noun phrase lists layers: زره (‘armor’) followed by زیر (‘under/beneath’) indicating position. بد qualifies جوشن (جوشن = cuirass/scale-armor) as ‘bad/poorly made’ or simply ‘ill-’ in front of جوشن. اندر میان literally ‘in the middle / between’ specifies the location of that cuirass (i.e. placed between other layers). Word order is modifier → head → locative.
segments.s222-g4: ازان پس is a temporal connector (‘from that / thereafter’). بپوشید again is past 3rd-person ‘he put on / he donned’. ببربیان is a following noun (likely the name of an item he then dons); its supplied senses do not match the occurrence, so its precise sense is treated as uncertain here.
segments.s222-g5: گرانمایه (literally ‘heavy/valuable in price’) modifies مغفر, here a head-covering or hood, so together ‘precious maghfar’. بسر in this line functions as a directional/locative element meaning ‘on the head’ (the supplied lemma senses are not a perfect fit, so its precise lexical sense is marked uncertain). بر نهاد is the compound past verb: بر (on/upon) + نهاد (past of نهادن ‘to place’), i.e. ‘placed on’. The clause means he placed the precious hood on his head.
segments.s222-g6: همی کرد is the classical imperfective/past habitual idiom: همی (marks continuous or customary action in epic Persian) + کرد (past of کردن) → ‘he would / he kept on’. بدخواهش is the subject of this verb phrase (‘his ill-wisher’ / enemy). از مرگ یاد is a small prepositional phrase ‘of/from death, remembrance’ with از functioning as ‘of/concerning’ and یاد as ‘remembrance’ — together with کرد it means ‘recalled/remembered death’. The whole clause: ‘his ill-wisher would constantly remember death.’
segments.s222-g7: بنیروی یزدان is an ablative/agent phrase ‘by (the) power of God’ (بِـ + نیروی = by the power of). میان را ببست uses میان (here ‘the mid/girth/waist area’ or ‘middle’) with the object marker را, and ببست is past 3rd-person of بستن meaning ‘he bound/tied/fastened’ — so the clause says ‘he fastened the middle/girth’ (i.e. tightened the girth).
segments.s222-g8: نشست is the past of نشستن here meaning ‘he sat / he mounted’. از بر رخش is the locative phrase ‘upon Rakhsh’ (از بر + name of the steed). چون پیل مست is a simile ‘like an elephant drunk/intoxicated’ (چون = like; پیل = elephant; مست = drunk), describing the manner in which he sat on his horse.
segments.s222-g105: بیامد is a simple past, third-person singular (‘he came’) placed first as a narrative verb. ببستش is past tense (ببست) with the enclitic object -ش ‘him’, so ‘he bound him/ran a tie around him’. بخم کمند is an instrumental/locative phrase: بخم (an archaic word meaning ‘at the bend/loop’) + کمند ‘lasso/noose’, literally ‘in the loop of the lasso’ and attaches to the verb ببستش to show how he bound him.
segments.s222-g106: بدو is the prepositional/enclitic phrase ‘to him’ (i.e., ‘said to him’). گفت here is the narrative past ‘said’. کاکنون (کاکنون) is an emphatic temporal adverb ‘now, at this moment’. شدی is second-person past of شدن used to indicate state/result (‘have become/are’). بی‌گزند is a predicate/adjective meaning ‘unharmed, safe’. Together the clause reports direct speech roughly: ‘he said to him, “Are you now unharmed?”’ (شدی marks the resulting state).
segments.s222-g107: ز تو is a prepositional phrase ‘because of you / from you’ that sets the cause. تنبل and جادوی دور are coordinated nominative/predicative nouns (‘laziness’ and ‘remote/distant magic’). گشت is the past/inchoative verb ‘became / took hold’, applying to the coordinated nouns: ‘(from you) laziness and distant sorcery came about.’
segments.s222-g108: روانت = روان + ـت, the possessed noun ‘your soul/spirit’. بر here is a preposition meaning roughly ‘to / into / under’ (marking the recipient/agent of the becoming). دیو مزدور is a noun phrase ‘demon hireling/mercenary’ (دیو modifying مزدور), and گشت is ‘became’. The construction says ‘your spirit became (turned) to/for the demon’s hireling’ (i.e. came under a demon’s service).
segments.s222-g109: سرآمد here is used as ‘end / conclusion’ (the outcome). بتو is the prepositional phrase ‘for you / upon you’. بر همه روز literally ‘over all days’ (a temporal phrase). کین (‘vengeance, enmity’) is the predicate/complement: the clause literally states ‘May the conclusion for you be enmity over all days’ (a curse/wish-like formula in context).
segments.s222-g110: نبینی is a present/future-oriented negative form ‘you will not see / you do not see’ (classical Persian allows this form for futurate). زمین کشانی is an agentive/epithet: زمین ‘land, country’ + کشانی (from کشاند/کشان) used as ‘one who drags or conquers lands’ = ‘conqueror of lands’. و چین adds ‘and China’ (چین as the place). The whole clause: ‘you will not see a conqueror of lands and [of] China.’
segments.s222-g111: گمان تو is ‘your belief / your thought’ (گمان with possessor تو). آن بد is a copular-like phrase ‘that (is) bad / mistaken’ (آن pointing to the belief and بد judging it). که introduces the subordinate clause (‘that …’). هنگام جنگ ‘at the time of war’ is the temporal adverbial placed before the subordinate clause’s predicate.
segments.s222-g112: کسی is an indefinite ‘someone / anyone’ (here understood in the negative sense from the whole sentence: ‘no one’ relative to your belief). چون تو is a comparative phrase ‘like you’. نگرفت is past tense of گرفتن ‘took/seized’ (here ‘seized/laid hold of’); in context it means ‘took up’ (a weapon). خنجر بچنگ is a small clause: خنجر ‘dagger’ + بچنگ (ب + چنگ) ‘into the hand/grasp’ meaning ‘took a dagger into the hand’ (i.e., ‘took up a dagger’).
segments.s222-g41: ورا (vora) here is the archaic object form 'او را' meaning 'him/it' and comes first for emphasis; دیده بودند is a compound past perfect ('دیده' past participle + بودند auxiliary) meaning 'had seen'. گردان نیو is a noun phrase following the verb; گردان is a substantive 'troop/company' and نیو is an attributive (here read as the regional/qualifying 'new'), so together they name the unit that was seen.
segments.s222-g42: چو begins a temporal/simile clause here ('when'/'as'), introducing the names that follow. طوس is the hero Tūs and سرافراز is an epithet ('triumphant / lofty') attached after the name. The coordinating و simply links the proper names رهام and گیو into the same series.
segments.s222-g43: کسی را نیامد is an idiomatic negative clause: کسی را with را topicalizes/ emphasizes 'no one', and نیامد is literal 'did not come' meaning 'no one came forward'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here ('indeed/at all'), and رزم رای is a noun phrase 'battle counsel/plan' (رزم = battle, رای = counsel/plan) that states what no one brought or proposed.
segments.s222-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the phrase 'from the troop of Iran'. گردان ایران is the genitival construction 'troop of Iran' and تهی ماند جای is a verbal phrase where تهی (empty, devoid) is predicative and ماند (past of ماندن) means 'remained/was left'; جای 'place/ranks' is the thing left empty. Word order places the prepositional origin first, then the comment that the place remained empty.
segments.s222-g45: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). با او 'with him' marks the contrastive companion, and کسی را نبد is an older negative copular construction: کسی را (topicalized 'no one') + نبد (old negative of بودن 'was not') = 'there was no one (to match him)'. تاو جنگ is a poetic phrase where تاو (radiance/brightness/renown) qualifies جنگ (battle), giving the sense 'in the brightness/glory of battle' or 'in battle.'
segments.s222-g46: This is a paired simile. دلیران (the brave/warriors) is the subject, چو + آهو creates the simile 'like gazelles.' The second half uses و او چون پلنگ 'and he like a leopard,' with او (he) as the contrasted subject and چون introducing the second simile. Word order is standard: subject — comparative particle — simile noun.
segments.s222-g47: یکی زابلی بود is a common introductory formula: یکی 'a certain one' (indefinite subject) + زابلی ('from Zabul') as a nisba/descriptive epithet, and بود 'was' is the past copula. الوای نام is an appositive inversion: 'named Alvây' (name following the noun), but the supplied lemma senses do not include a proper‑name reading, so the name is treated as an appositive here.
segments.s222-g48: سبک تیغ is a noun phrase where سبک (light in weight) modifies تیغ (sword). کین is a genitive modifier 'of vengeance' attached to تیغ (a 'sword of vengeance'). برکشید is a simple past verb 'drew up/raised' (here 'drew [a weapon]'), and از نیام is the prepositional complement 'from the sheath/scabbard' (from + نیام).
segments.s222-g81: This chunk begins with the temporal adverb کنون ("now"). رشته ("thread/cord") is the head noun and خوانی here is a dependent element immediately following it (a poetic/compound use: the noun + a following verbal/nominal element), together pointing to the ‘thread/strand’ of a snare; کمند ("noose/snare") specifies the kind of cord. مرا is the speaker’s object (me) formed from the enclitic م + object marker را split in the data; the whole noun phrase is thus ‘the cord of my snare’ placed before the comment that follows.
segments.s222-g82: ببینی is a 2nd-person present-form meaning “you see” (here used as an assertive/perceptual verb). همی is the archaic/poetic continuous/emphatic particle (‘indeed/just’ or marking ongoing action) that often precedes or follows the verb in Ferdowsi’s style. تنگ و بند is a coordinated adjective+noun (“tight and bond/fetter”) describing the effect on the speaker; مرا (m + را) is again the object pronoun ‘me’ (object marker ra attached), so the clause reads ‘you see indeed my tightening and bondage.’
segments.s222-g83: زمانه (here used as ‘fate/fortune/the world’) is the subject. ترا is the 2nd-person object formed in the data as ت + را (the old enclitic order), so ‘fate’ acts upon ‘you’. از کشانی is an ablative/agentive phrase built from از + the verbal noun/participle کشانی (from کشاندن ‘to drag/bring’), translated roughly ‘by/through dragging’, and براند is the present/epic-present verb ‘drives away’; syntactically the verb comes at the end as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s222-g84: چو here functions as a temporal/concessive conjunction ‘when’ (or ‘as’). ایدر means ‘here’; بدت is بد + ت = ‘bad/unfavourable for you’ (the -t marking second-person possession/affectedness). خاک جایت is a genitive/possessive construction ‘the earth of your place’ (خاک = earth, جا + -ت = your place). نماند is the negative/resultative verb ‘will not remain/there will be none left.’ Together: ‘when here your lot is bad, the earth of your place will not remain.’
segments.s222-g85: برانگیخت is a past/epic past verb meaning ‘he aroused/roused’ and stands first for emphasis. کاموس here appears as a proper name or epithet placed before اسپ (horse/steed), so the phrase reads roughly ‘he roused Kamus’s war-steed’ or ‘he roused the war-steed, Kamus.’ نبرد (literally ‘battle’) qualifies the horse as a war/warrior’s mount; the word order (verb — name — noun — qualifier) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s222-g86: هم here means ‘also’ or ‘additionally’. آورد is the past form ‘he brought’ (or ‘was bringing’ in epic narrative). را is the definite/object marker attaching to the following object. دید is the past of ‘to see’ (‘he saw’). با + دارو (with medicine/remedy) is an instrumental phrase ‘with a remedy/medicine’. برد in this poetic line functions as a past action ‘carried off/took away’ (note: برد can also mean ‘victory’ or ‘gain’ in the dictionary list, but here it continues the narrative: he carried/took). The clause therefore links: ‘he also saw what was brought and took it with a remedy.’
segments.s222-g87: بینداخت is the past tense ‘threw/cast’ (the prefix بیـ is part of the compound verb form here). تیغ (sword) is the direct object that follows the verb in epic style. پرند is an appositive or epithet meaning ‘winged one/bird’ (here likely a warrior or messenger described as winged) and آورش combines آور + ش (bring + his) meaning ‘his bringer’ or ‘the one who brought him/it’; together the phrase describes who was involved with the sword’s motion: ‘he hurled the sword at/against the bird-bringer.’
segments.s222-g88: همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle (here accompanying a past wishing/attempt verb). خواست is a past-tense verb ‘he wanted / attempted’. از تن بریدن is an infinitive construction where از + تن + بریدن = ‘to cut off from the body’ (بریدن here means specifically ‘to behead/sever’). سرش (سر + ش) is the possessed noun ‘his head.’ Grammatically the infinitive phrase is the object of خواست: ‘he sought/attempted to cut off his head from his body.’
segments.s222-g89: This clause has the (poetic) subject phrase 'سر تیغ' (the head/tip of the sword) before the verb. 'بر' here functions as a spatial preposition 'on/upon' and attaches to 'گردن رخش' ('Rakhsh's neck'). 'خورد' is the perfective/past sense 'befell/struck' (classical usage meaning 'hit'). Word order is S — Prep phrase — V, typical for Persian poetry.
segments.s222-g90: This short clause continues the action: 'ببرید' functions here as a past perfective meaning 'it cut (off)'. The sequence lacks an explicit subject (understood as the sword), a common elliptic pattern. The following words 'برگستوان نبرد' are compressed/archaic: 'برگستوان' is a fused/poetic form and 'نبرد' can be read as a nominal or verbal root related to the fight; the clause as a whole should be taken as reporting the cutting action in the context of battle.
segments.s222-g91: 'تن رخش را' places the definite object 'Rakhsh's body' first and marks it with the object marker 'را'. 'زان' means 'from that' or 'by that' (referring back to the previous blow). 'نیامد' is the simple past negation 'did not come' and with 'گزند' (harm) yields the idiom 'زان نیامد گزند' = 'no harm came from that.' The clause thus states a negative result.
segments.s222-g92: 'گو' here introduces a name or epithet and functions like 'called'/'named' (classical formulaic usage). 'پیلتن' is a proper name/place. 'حلقه کرد' is a compound verb meaning 'made (into) a ring/loop' (حلقه = ring; کرد = made). 'آن کمند' is the object 'that lasso/snare' preceded by the demonstrative 'آن' (that). The clause reads 'the one called Peiltan made that lasso into a loop.'
segments.s222-g93: 'بینداخت و افگندش' uses two near-synonymous classical verbs 'بینداخت' and 'افگند' (both 'threw/cast') in sequence for emphasis; the second carries the clitic object '-ش' = 'it'. 'اندر میان' is a prepositional phrase 'into the midst/among them'. The coordination with 'و' links the throwing action to the following one.
segments.s222-g94: 'برانگیخت' is a past/telic verb 'he roused/raised'. It takes the source phrase 'از جای' = 'from the place (of)' and the compound 'پیل ژیان' designates an elephant (literally 'elephant of life' in older diction, here 'the elephant'). The clause therefore means 'he roused the elephant from its place.'
segments.s222-g95: 'بزین اندر آورد' is a compact classical phrase: 'بزین' (saddle up / to the saddle) + 'اندر آورد' ('brought into') = 'brought (him) into the saddle'. The conjunction 'و' links to 'کردش دوال': 'کردش' = 'made him' (object clitic), and 'دوال' is an archaic adjective/noun meaning 'twofold' or 'paired' — here a poetic way of saying he was doubled or enhanced. Word order is verb–object clitic typical of classical Persian.
segments.s222-g96: 'عقابی شده' is a stative perfect meaning 'has become eagle-like' (عقابی as an adjective 'eagle-like'). 'رخش' remains the subject 'Rakhsh (the horse)'. The prepositional phrase 'با پر و بال' 'with feather and wing(s)' describes the new, eagle-like attributes. The order is predicate before subject, a frequent poetic inversion.
segments.s222-g129: کفن شد کنون مغفر و جوشنش is a poetic word order where شد (become) is the copula/verb and مغفر و جوشنش ('his maghfar and cuirass') is the predicate that has become the کفن (shroud). کنون ('now') is a temporal adverb placed after the verb as often in classical Persian; the possessive clitic -ش attaches to جوشن but implies possession of both items.
segments.s222-g130: ز خاک افسر و گرد پیراهنش uses ز as a preposition meaning 'with/from' indicating the material or covering; افسر ('helmet') and گرد پیراهنش ('the dust/ruffles of his tunic') are the things afflicted by that covering. گرد here is a noun 'dust' (not a verb), و coordinates the two items, and the possessive -ش on پیراهن marks 'his tunic'.
segments.s222-g131: شما را بکشتن چگونست رای places the infinitive/masdar بکشتن ('to kill') together with the object شما را ('you' with the object marker را) before چگونست رای ('how is the counsel/what is the plan'). چگونست is an archaic fused form meaning 'how is it' (چگونه است) and رای is the noun 'counsel/plan', so the whole clause asks 'what is the plan for killing you?'.
segments.s222-g132: که شد کار کاموس جنگی ز پای is a subordinate clause introduced by که used causally ('for/because'), شد is the past copula 'became/happened', and کار is the subject 'affair/doing'. کاموس جنگی is a proper name plus epithet 'Kamus the warrior' (the supplied lemma senses do not include a proper-name sense), and ز پای is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'to an end/disabled' with ز as the preposition 'from' and پای here 'end/limit'.
segments.s222-g133: بیفگند بر خاک پیش سران is a narrative past verb form (بیفگند) meaning 'cast/threw' with its object omitted or earlier in context; بر خاک ('onto the ground') is the prepositional phrase marking where, and پیش سران ('in front of the chiefs') specifies the place/people present. In epic diction the verb often precedes its subject, which appears in the next segment.
segments.s222-g134: ز لشکر برفتند کنداوران places the verb برفتند ('they departed/went away') before its noun subject کنداوران ('the diggers/excavators'); ز لشکر ('from the army/host') gives the source from which they left. The order (verb then subject) is typical of the poem's style.
segments.s222-g135: تنش را بشمشیر کردند چاک combines the object phrase تنش را ('his body' with the object marker را and possessive clitic -ش) with the instrumental phrase بشمشیر ('with the sword') and the verb کردند ('they did/made'). چاک ('a gash' or 'it gaped') is the resulting noun/complement describing the wound; classic Persian often places the result after the verb.
segments.s222-g136: بخون غرقه شد زیر او سنگ و خاک has بخون here as a prepositional/qualifying unit meaning 'in blood' (written together in the verse), غرقه ('drenched/immersed') is an adjective and شد the copula 'became'. زیر او ('under him') marks location and سنگ و خاک ('stone and earth') are the things lying beneath him. Note: the lemma entry given for 'بخون' in the gloss list is an imperative form, so the appropriate sense for the phrase 'in blood' is not among the provided senses.
segments.s222-g121: چنین است is a copular phrase ('thus is / it is thus') placed before the noun phrase for emphasis. رسم سرای فریب is a nominal phrase: رسم 'custom' governs سرای فریب 'the house/abode of deception' (سرای = 'house/abode' + فریب = 'deception'); Persian poetry often omits an explicit ezāfe or rearranges word order for rhythm, so the whole noun phrase functions as the predicate being described by چنین است.
segments.s222-g122: گهی ... و گهی ... is a repeated adverbial construction meaning 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. Each گهی introduces a prepositional phrase (در فراز = 'in/on the heights', در نشیب = 'in the descent/low point'); these phrases modify the preceding clause (they describe how the 'custom' shows itself). The coordination و simply links the alternating adverbials.
segments.s222-g123: بایران (the barren lands) is the topical subject; همی شد is a classical/periphrastic habitual/past phrase ('it came to pass repeatedly / it so happened') where همی gives a habitual or iterative flavor and شد = 'happened / became'. که introduces the content clause that follows. ویران کند uses کند in the same classical/habitual sense ('would make/laid waste'); the object of 'make ruined' is understood from context.
segments.s222-g124: کنام here ('konam') is a noun meaning 'bosom/lair' and functions predicatively with کند ('becomes / makes') to say that the barren places became the haunts of wild beasts. پلنگان و شیران is a coordinated genitive/possessive expression 'of the leopards and of the lions' (their lairs). The repeated کند carries the same modal/habitual sense as in the previous clause.
segments.s222-g125: به + place names is a prepositional phrase indicating location ('in/to Zabulistan and to/in Kabulistan'); the two places are simply listed and linked by و. In poetry به is frequently used before place names to mean 'in' or 'in the region of'.
segments.s222-g126: نه ... و نه ... is the paired negation 'neither ... nor ...'. Here the pattern places the nominal (ایوان) before the copula بود ('was/there was'), a common poetic word order: 'no iwan was likewise and no garden' → 'there was neither an iwan nor a garden.' نیز is functioning as 'also/either' in the negative construction.
segments.s222-g127: نیندازد is a negative verbal form ('does not throw / will not let go'); از دست literally 'from the hand' expresses separation or loss of control, and گوپال را is the definite/direct object marked by را. Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before its object phrase) as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s222-g128: مگر introduces an exception 'except / unless'. The clause گم کند رستم زال را places the verb phrase 'گم کند' ('would lose / causes to be lost' in the classical/habitual-subjunctive sense) before its subject رستم (inverted order) and its object زال را (Zāl with the object marker). The construction as a whole yields 'unless Rostam should/was to lose Zāl.'
segments.s222-g17: This is a nominal phrase in which the head is لشکر آرای هند (“the army that arrays/ornaments India” or more naturally “the Indian‑arrayed host”) and the two preceding words ابر میسره act as poetic modifiers — ابر (‘cloud’) used metaphorically and میسره as an epithet attached before the head. Word order is modifier + head (typical in Persian poetry), so treat the whole string as one extended noun phrase.
segments.s222-g18: زره‌دار is an adjective/epithet ‘armored’ (or an armored warrior) modifying the host; با introduces an accompaniment/means ‘with’; تیغ is the instrument ‘sword’; و = ‘and’; هندی modifies پرند, so ‘Indian‑plumed’ or ‘Indians with plumes’ — the phrase strings adjectives and complements after the noun to describe the troops. Note the taut, paratactic listing typical of epic description.
segments.s222-g19: This is a prepositional/locative chunk: بقلب اندرون جای خاقان = ‘in the inner heart/place of the khagan’. بقلب + اندرون reinforce location (‘in the heart / inner part’), جای names the locus ‘place/seat’, and خاقان is the possessor ‘of the khagan’. The sequence is inward → place → possessor, a compacted locative phrase common in poetic diction.
segments.s222-g20: شده is a perfect/passive/predicative participle ‘has become / was made’ placed before the subject(s) in a poetic inversion; following it are two short predications: آسمان تار (‘the sky [became] dark’) and جنبان زمین (‘and the earth [became] moving/agitated’). In normal prose the order would be آسمان تار شده و زمین جنبان شده, but poetry permits the verb up front.
segments.s222-g21: وزین رو is an adjectival phrase ‘with a heavy/dignified face/appearance’ placed before the name for emphasis. فریبرز is the proper name; بر میسره is a locative / position phrase ‘on/at the mi‑sere’ (بر = on/upon + میسره as the place or seat). This follows Persian order adjective → noun → locative.
segments.s222-g22: چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’; خورشید تابان is the compared image ‘the shining sun’. ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’; برج بره literally ‘the tower/constellation of the Ram’ — in epic usage برج بره points to the zodiacal sign Aries (hence a rising, auspicious sun). The clause is a compact simile comparing Fereburz’s appearance to the sun rising in Aries.
segments.s222-g23: سوی میمنه is a locative phrase ‘to the right’; پور کشواد is a name construction ‘the son of Kashvād’ (پور is the patronymic element ‘son’); بود is the past copula ‘was’. The order is locative → noun (name) → copula, yielding ‘to the right was Kashvād’s son’.
segments.s222-g24: که introduces a relative clause ‘whose…’; کتفش (‘his shoulder’) is the possessed noun; همه زیر پولاد بود = ‘was entirely under steel’, where همه ‘entirely/whole’ modifies the following phrase, زیر ‘under/beneath’ governs پولاد ‘steel’ (i.e. covered by steel/armored), and بود is the past copula. The clause describes his shoulder as fully sheathed in steel.

sentences.s222-s69: Do not, in manliness, fall into the belief that Time's hand is long against you.
sentences.s222-s70: This battle came to an end (Kāmos-gird); so it happened that life was brought and life was taken.
sentences.s222-s33: He struck with the spear and seized him from the saddle, casting him easily onto the ground.
sentences.s222-s34: He tightened the reins and struck him with a horseshoe, beating him continually until his dust became ruby.
sentences.s222-s35: Tahmēntan, startled by the cry, became distressed; he unfastened the coiling lasso from the saddle-cloth.
sentences.s222-s36: When (you) made for battle against the chiefs, you carried a lasso and a heavy mace.
sentences.s222-s13: In the inner heart of Tūs Nowzar stood ready, at the head of the army with drum and trumpet sounding.
sentences.s222-s14: Smoke and fire rose from the water; one does not see such a battle in sleep.
sentences.s222-s15: From every side rose the army's uproar; that (noise) tore the elephant's ears.
sentences.s222-s16: First, the one who came between the two ranks, with foam on his lip brought from the blood of his liver.
sentences.s222-s17: The triumphant spahbed was Kamus, who stood with an army, elephants, and war‑drums.
sentences.s222-s18: He thundered forth like a drunken elephant; one, bull‑built, held a mace in his hand.
sentences.s222-s19: Where is that foot‑warrior? — who, of the famed, asked for such a fight.
sentences.s222-s20: If now he comes to the battlefield, the place will remain empty of his arrow.
sentences.s222-s29: Do not be overwhelmed by the waters of your own skills; keep your footing in your place.
sentences.s222-s30: Like a drop cast into the deep sea, this judgment remains madness.
sentences.s222-s31: A cry proclaiming Kamus’s intent arose, for he sought to bring battle against him.
sentences.s222-s32: They set up a great battlefield; a force came, behaving like wolves.
sentences.s222-s57: May it not be that vengeance makes him proud, for he will very soon see the ups and downs.
sentences.s222-s58: He bound his two hands behind his back like a stone; into the loop of the snare he brought a claw.
sentences.s222-s59: An army came striding (stately) from Bayrān; he dragged the avenger down to the ground.
sentences.s222-s60: Turning, the vengeance-seeking warrior said thus: 'Because of such strength and hatred it came upon you.'
sentences.s222-s49: The rider, by his daring, pressed the flank; the rider grew heavy and the reins grew light.
sentences.s222-s50: He kept trying to undo that bend of the raw noose; with force he strove to tear it apart from its bond.
sentences.s222-s51: He lost consciousness; the raw (piece) tore, and (one called) Piltan tamed Rakhsh.
sentences.s222-s52: He snatched the reins and from the saddle pulled him down; he overthrew him and struck him upon the ground.
sentences.s222-s37: He came and roared like an intoxicated elephant, a lasso on his arm and a mace in his hand.
sentences.s222-s38: Kāmos said to him for a while: 'By the power of this rope there are sixty coils.'
sentences.s222-s39: Rostam made this reply: when a lion sees its prey, he bellows bravely.
sentences.s222-s40: First you girded this grudge about you; you killed one famed man from Iran.
sentences.s222-s25: Where did you hold Rostam's spear? You left him no rear (no support) at all.
sentences.s222-s26: He has borne great hardship, learned the use of the reins, and mastered the mace, the arrow and the spear.
sentences.s222-s27: With sorrow and severe hardship, his heart burning, he learned the arts from Rostam.
sentences.s222-s28: Rostam said to him, 'Be awake; bring this Turk — be on your guard.'
sentences.s222-s5: Because of his height the sky stood aghast; the earth, in the wake of his steed, grew dark.
sentences.s222-s6: From both hosts arose trumpet and drum; the earth turned to iron, the heavens to ebony.
sentences.s222-s7: The world trembled and was shaken; plain and mountain — the earth was wearied by the hoofs of the herds.
sentences.s222-s8: On his right the dignified-faced Kamus; behind him a ragged elephant and a small heap.
sentences.s222-s1: Rostam donned his war-gear and went to the place of combat, with darubard at hand.
sentences.s222-s2: Beneath the armor he placed a poor cuirass in the middle; after that he put on the babr-biyān.
sentences.s222-s3: He set a precious maghfar upon his head; his ill-wisher continually remembered death.
sentences.s222-s4: By the power of God he fastened the girth, and he sat upon Rakhsh like a drunken elephant.
sentences.s222-s53: He came, bound him in the bend of the lasso, and said to him, “Are you now unharmed?”
sentences.s222-s54: Because of you, laziness and distant sorcery took hold; your spirit became the hireling of a demon.
sentences.s222-s55: May your end be, for you, enmity above all days; you will not see a conqueror of lands and of China.
sentences.s222-s56: Your idea is wrong: that at the time of battle no one like you took a dagger in hand.
sentences.s222-s21: They had seen him there — the new troop — when Tous, triumphant, and Roham and Giv.
sentences.s222-s22: No one came forward with counsel for the battle; the ranks of Iran stood empty.
sentences.s222-s23: For with him there was no one in the fight; the brave were like gazelles, and he like a leopard.
sentences.s222-s24: A certain man from Zabul there was, named Alvây; he drew the light sword of vengeance from its sheath.
sentences.s222-s41: Now behold the cord of my snare; you see how it tightens and binds me.
sentences.s222-s42: Fate will drive you away as if dragging you; when your lot is ill here, there will be no earth left for your place.
sentences.s222-s43: He roused (or spurred) the war-steed; he also saw what was brought and took it (or bore it) with a remedy.
sentences.s222-s44: He hurled the sword; the winged one (the bird-bringer) flung it — he then tried to sever his head from his body.
sentences.s222-s45: The sword's head struck Rakhsh's neck; it cut (off), yet the arm did not leave the fight.
sentences.s222-s46: Rakhsh's body was not harmed by that; so one called Peiltan made that lasso into a loop.
sentences.s222-s47: He hurled and cast it into the midst, and raised the elephant from its place.
sentences.s222-s48: He put him into the saddle and made him twofold; Rakhsh became eagle-like, with feathers and wings.
sentences.s222-s65: Now his maghfar and cuirass became his shroud; his helmet and the folds of his tunic were covered with earth and dust.
sentences.s222-s66: What counsel is there to kill you? — for the warlike Kamus's affairs have been put to an end.
sentences.s222-s67: They cast it down on the ground before the chiefs; the grave-diggers departed from the host.
sentences.s222-s68: They cut his body with the sword so that it gaped; he became drenched in blood, and stone and earth lay beneath him.
sentences.s222-s61: Such is the custom of the house of deception: sometimes on high and sometimes in the low.
sentences.s222-s62: It so happened to the barren lands that they were laid waste and became the lairs of leopards and lions.
sentences.s222-s63: In Zabulistan and in Kabulistan there was neither an iwan nor a garden.
sentences.s222-s64: He would not let Gōpāl go from his hand, unless Rostam were to lose Zāl.
sentences.s222-s9: A cloudlike Mi-sereh, the India‑arrayed host, armored, with swords and Indians wearing plumes.
sentences.s222-s10: In the inner heart/place of the khagan there was a fold — the sky grew dark and the earth trembled.
sentences.s222-s11: And with a dignified visage Fereburz on the mi‑sere, like the shining sun from the sign of the Ram (Aries).
sentences.s222-s12: To the right was Kashvād’s son; his shoulder was entirely beneath steel.
